threats.title: Whitelist Bypass: Unwhitelisted Addresses Pass checkWhitelist Due to Default Mapping Value
threats.hypothesis_description: The checkWhitelist function uses the condition `_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` to revert for non-whitelisted users. However, unwhitelisted addresses have a default _whitelistIndex of 0. When _allowedWhitelistIndex is set to any value > 0 (normal operation), the check `0 > N` evaluates to false, so the revert is never triggered for unwhitelisted addresses. This means ANY address can buy tokens from the Uniswap V3 pool during the whitelist period, completely nullifying the whitelist mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The logic flaw is in the boolean condition. The intended behavior is to allow only addresses with index 1..._allowedWhitelistIndex. The missing check is `_whitelistIndex[to] == 0` which would catch unwhitelisted addresses. This is a clear code defect observable from the source.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Default mapping value 0 for _whitelistIndex[to] bypasses `_whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` check when _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0

preconditions: _allowedWhitelistIndex is set to a value > 0 (normal whitelist operation)
_locked is false (whitelist period active but not fully locked)
Transfer originates from the Uniswap pool (_pool)
Recipient is not the contract owner

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _whitelistIndex[to] defaults to 0 for unwhitelisted addresses; 0 > N is always false for N > 0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check for _whitelistIndex[to] == 0 to catch unwhitelisted addresses

analysis_questions: Confirm that _whitelistIndex is only set via _addWhitelistedAddress and starts at 1
Verify no other code path sets _whitelistIndex for arbitrary addresses
Confirm that unwhitelisted addresses always have _whitelistIndex == 0
validation_requirements: Deploy Whitelist, set _allowedWhitelistIndex to 5, verify unwhitelisted address passes checkWhitelist
Verify whitelisted address with index 3 also passes (correct behavior)
Verify address with index 6 correctly reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker buys VULT tokens from Uniswap V3 pool during whitelist period without being whitelisted. The _maxAddressCap check still applies but the whitelist gate is completely bypassed.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete whitelist bypass allows unlimited non-whitelisted addresses to purchase tokens, destroying fair launch mechanics
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All legitimate whitelisted participants who expected restricted access
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None during active period; damage is immediate

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for unwhitelisted address attempting purchase during active whitelist period

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring purchases from non-whitelisted addresses

threats.title: Missing Upper Price Range Validation in initILOPool Allows Invalid Liquidity Position Creation
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.initILOPool only validates `sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96` but does NOT validate `_project.initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96`. If initialPoolPriceX96 >= sqrtRatioUpperX96, the concentrated liquidity position is entirely in one token. The buy() and launch() functions use _saleAmountNeeded which calculates liquidity based on the assumption that price is within range, producing incorrect values. Investors purchase positions with miscalculated liquidity, and launch may fail or deploy incorrect token ratios.
threats.investigation_rationale: Valid concentrated liquidity requires the current price to be strictly within the tick range. The RANGE check in initILOPool is incomplete—it only enforces the lower bound. This is a clear missing validation in the contract code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96 && sqrtRatioLowerX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96, 'RANGE') — missing initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96

preconditions: Project admin creates ILO pool with tickUpper such that sqrtRatioUpperX96 <= initialPoolPriceX96
The RANGE require check passes because lower < initial AND lower < upper

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Only lower bound of price range is validated
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If price >= upper tick, getLiquidityForAmount0/1 in _saleAmountNeeded produces incorrect liquidity calculations

analysis_questions: What exact values does getLiquidityForAmount0/1 return when price >= upper tick?
Does the internal swap of A,B in LiquidityAmounts produce a valid but wrong result?
Can launch succeed with these wrong values, or does it revert?
validation_requirements: Create ILO pool with price at or above upper tick, verify RANGE check passes
Attempt buy() and verify liquidity calculations are incorrect
Attempt launch() and verify behavior

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Project admin (malicious or mistaken) sets tickUpper such that sqrtRatioUpperX96 < initialPoolPriceX96. ILO pool is created. Investors buy positions with incorrectly calculated liquidity. At launch, addLiquidity deploys with wrong token ratios, potentially requiring more SALE_TOKEN than available (revert) or deploying an under-collateralized position.
prerequisites: Project admin sets invalid tick parameters
Investors deposit RAISE_TOKEN into the pool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Investors receive positions with incorrect liquidity amounts; launch may fail permanently locking funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All investors in the affected ILO pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Refund path exists if launch fails, but requires waiting for refundDeadline

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for price at or above upper tick boundary

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by verifying price range parameters at pool creation

threats.title: Zero-Duration Vest Schedule Causes Permanent Division-by-Zero DoS on Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: _validateVestSchedule does not check that schedule[i].start < schedule[i].end. If a vesting entry has start == end, the _unlockedLiquidity function's else branch computes (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS = 0 * 10000 = 0 as the denominator in FullMath.mulDiv, causing a division by zero revert. This permanently DoSes all claim() calls for affected positions, locking vested liquidity forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation in _validateVestSchedule only checks schedule[i].start >= lastEnd and share totals. There is no requirement that start < end. When block.timestamp == start == end, the condition `vest.end < block.timestamp` is false, falling into the else branch with zero denominator.

proof_of_signal.pattern: else branch: FullMath.mulDiv(vest.shares * totalLiquidity, block.timestamp - vest.start, (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS) with zero denominator when start==end

preconditions: Project admin creates ILO pool with a vest schedule entry where start == end
_validateVestSchedule does not reject it
Pool launches successfully
block.timestamp reaches vest.start (== vest.end)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No start < end validation in _validateVestSchedule; division by (vest.end - vest.start) in _unlockedLiquidity
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Zero denominator causes permanent revert in claim path

analysis_questions: Can a zero-duration vest pass all validation checks?
Does the DoS affect only the specific position or all positions in the pool?
Is there any recovery mechanism once this state is reached?
validation_requirements: Create ILO pool with start == end in vest schedule, verify it passes validation
Attempt claim after launch, verify revert
Confirm no alternative path to recover funds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious project admin creates ILO pool with vest schedule containing start == end. Investors deposit RAISE_TOKEN. Pool launches successfully. When claim time arrives, all claims revert due to division by zero. Investor liquidity is permanently locked in Uniswap V3.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all vested liquidity for affected positions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All investors in the affected ILO pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - no alternative claim/withdrawal mechanism exists

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for vest schedule with start == end

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Permanent DoS once vest start time is reached
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by inspecting vest schedule parameters at pool creation

threats.title: launch() Sets _launchSucceeded After External Calls Creating Reentrancy Window
threats.hypothesis_description: In ILOPool.launch(), _launchSucceeded is set to true as the LAST operation, after addLiquidity (external call to Uniswap), minting project NFTs, and _refundProject (external call transferring SALE_TOKEN). If SALE_TOKEN has transfer callbacks (e.g., ERC777 or custom hook), the callback executes while _launchSucceeded is still false. If ILOManager owner has set refundDeadline <= block.timestamp, the refundable() modifier would pass, potentially allowing state corruption. Even without callbacks, this violates the checks-effects-interactions pattern.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _launchSucceeded flag is the critical state gate for multiple functions (claim requires true, claimRefund requires false). Setting it after external calls creates a window where the contract state is inconsistent with its actual lifecycle.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _launchSucceeded = true is the last statement; _refundProject makes external safeTransfer call before this

preconditions: SALE_TOKEN has transfer callback capability (ERC777, custom hook)
refundDeadline <= block.timestamp at launch time (requires ILOManager owner manipulation or past-due deadline)
Sufficient totalRaised >= softCap

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: _refundProject(_project.admin) external call at line 266; _launchSucceeded = true at line 268
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reentrancy via SALE_TOKEN callback while _launchSucceeded is false
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reentrancy guard; _launchSucceeded not set before external calls

analysis_questions: Can claimRefund be called during the callback window?
What happens if _refundTriggered is set during launch's external call?
Are RAISE_TOKENs still in the contract at that point?
validation_requirements: Deploy with callback-capable SALE_TOKEN, verify reentrancy is possible
Test if claimRefund can execute during launch callback
Verify state consistency after reentrancy attempt

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ILOManager owner sets refundDeadline to launchTime. At launch time, launch() is called. During _refundProject's SALE_TOKEN transfer callback, attacker calls claimRefund. _launchSucceeded is false, refundDeadline has passed. RAISE_TOKEN was already sent to Uniswap via addLiquidity, so refund transfer would fail. However, state corruption (setting _refundTriggered = true) may cause issues.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential state corruption of launch/refund mutex; RAISE_TOKEN already deployed to Uniswap
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on extent of state corruption

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing reentrancy test during launch with callback tokens

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Callback + reentry gas within block limit
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable via internal transaction trace showing reentry

threats.title: Liquidity Accounting Mismatch Between Per-User buy() Calculations and Aggregate launch() Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: buy() calculates each user's liquidity individually: getLiquidity(raiseAmount_i) * shares / BPS with floor division at each step. launch() calculates total liquidity from totalRaised: getLiquidity(totalRaised). Due to the non-linearity of liquidity calculations and accumulated rounding errors from floor division, sum(individual liquidities) likely does not equal the total position liquidity multiplied by investor shares / BPS. If sum(individual) > actual_investor_share, late claimers' burn() calls will revert when the Uniswap position has insufficient remaining liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: getLiquidityForAmount0/1 involves intermediate divisions that lose precision. When applied N times to small amounts, accumulated rounding differs from a single calculation on the aggregate. The liquidity deployed to Uniswap is based on the aggregate, but claims attempt to burn the sum of individual calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buy() per-user: getLiquidity(raiseAmount) * shares / BPS; launch() aggregate: getLiquidity(totalRaised); accumulated rounding in floor division

preconditions: Multiple investors buy into the pool with varying amounts
Pool launches successfully
Rounding accumulates sufficiently that sum(individual_liquidity) > actual_investor_liquidity_in_uniswap

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-user floor division vs aggregate calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: sum(getLiquidity(amount_i) * shares / BPS) may exceed getLiquidity(sum(amount_i)) * shares / BPS
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reconciliation step between individual and aggregate liquidity calculations

analysis_questions: What is the maximum possible rounding discrepancy for realistic parameters?
Does the discrepancy always favor individual calculations (over-counting)?
How many investors are needed for the mismatch to cause a revert?
validation_requirements: Simulate 100+ buys with varying amounts, compare sum of liquidities to aggregate
Attempt full claim cycle for all positions, verify if last claimer reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 100 investors each buy 1 ETH. Sum of individual liquidity calculations exceeds the actual Uniswap liquidity by N units. When the last few investors try to claim their full vested liquidity, the Uniswap burn reverts due to insufficient remaining liquidity in the position.
prerequisites: Multiple investors with varying purchase amounts
Rounding accumulation sufficient to cause discrepancy

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Last claimers permanently lose some or all vested liquidity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Late claimers in pools with many investors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial - some liquidity may still be claimable

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing comprehensive multi-user rounding reconciliation test

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing sum of position liquidities to Uniswap position liquidity

threats.title: ILOPool.buy uses ERC721Enumerable function without inheriting it, potentially bricking buys or enabling inconsistent tokenId selection
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy() calls tokenOfOwnerByIndex(recipient, 0) to retrieve an existing position tokenId, but ILOPool inherits OZ ERC721 (not ERC721Enumerable) in the provided code. If ERC721Enumerable is not actually present in the compiled inheritance tree, buy() cannot compile or will revert/behave unexpectedly. Even if a custom ERC721 implementation is used elsewhere, relying on enumeration for a 'single position per user' rule can be fragile under transfers/burns, causing tokenId selection mismatches and incorrect position updates.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect signal: a function call to an extension that is not visible in inheritance. If true, the main sale entrypoint fails entirely or exhibits inconsistent tokenId resolution (breaking accounting invariants like totalRaised consistency).
deployment_assumptions: The deployed ILOPool bytecode matches the shown source (inherits OZ ERC721 without ERC721Enumerable)
No custom ERC721 implementation providing tokenOfOwnerByIndex is used at compile time
threats.config_evidence: No deployment artifact/flattened source proving ERC721Enumerable inclusion was provided; code shows `import '@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC721/ERC721.sol';` only.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buy() calls tokenOfOwnerByIndex(recipient, 0) but contract inherits ERC721 (no enumerable extension shown)

preconditions: Recipient already owns an ILOPool NFT so the else-branch executes
tokenOfOwnerByIndex is not implemented (or is implemented inconsistently with expected semantics)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: buy() may be non-functional or may select wrong tokenId for position updates
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative mapping from owner=>tokenId; no explicit enumeration inheritance in provided code

analysis_questions: Does ILOPool actually inherit ERC721Enumerable or an equivalent implementation in the real compilation unit?
If enumerable is present, can tokenOfOwnerByIndex(recipient,0) ever return an unexpected tokenId after transfers/burns causing position/accounting corruption?
Is the intended invariant 'one token per recipient' enforced elsewhere (e.g., transfer hooks) or purely by balanceOf==0 check?
validation_requirements: Confirm compiled inheritance tree and ABI contains tokenOfOwnerByIndex
If present, fuzz transfers + multiple buys to ensure tokenId selection always targets the intended single position
Add unit test ensuring buy() works after NFT transfers, burns (refund), and re-minting

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker (or normal user) with an existing position triggers buy() else-branch; if tokenOfOwnerByIndex is missing or broken, buy() reverts, halting further contributions and potentially preventing reaching softCap. If it returns an unintended tokenId, the contract may credit liquidity/raiseAmount to the wrong position, desyncing totalRaised vs sum(raiseAmount).
prerequisites: At least one position NFT exists for a recipient

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Sale can be permanently or intermittently bricked; or accounting may corrupt leading to incorrect refunds/claims
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All buyers after first mint to a recipient; potentially all participants if buys halt

threats.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of tests covering second buy() for same recipient and enumeration behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: ABI/inheritance verification + runtime tests on deployed bytecode

threats.title: Whitelist.receive() refunds ETH via transfer() causing self-whitelist DoS for contract wallets and potentially blocking whitelist growth
threats.hypothesis_description: Whitelist.receive() self-whitelists sender then refunds msg.value using `payable(_msgSender()).transfer(msg.value)`, which forwards only 2300 gas. Contract wallets (multisigs, smart accounts) with non-trivial receive/fallback logic will revert on transfer, preventing self-whitelisting for those users. This can be exploited as a griefing/availability issue for legitimate participants who rely on smart accounts, and can also become a systemic DoS if most users are contract wallets.
threats.investigation_rationale: Use of `transfer` is a known fragility due to gas stipend changes and contract-wallet incompatibility. Since self-whitelisting is explicitly a feature, breaking it is a functional/security availability defect during the launch phase.
deployment_assumptions: Self-whitelist is enabled (_isSelfWhitelistDisabled == false) during a period when contract-wallet users are expected to participate
threats.config_evidence: Whitelist.sol exposes setIsSelfWhitelistDisabled(bool) but no deployment/setup evidence was provided for its intended runtime value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: refund uses payable(msg.sender).transfer(msg.value) (2300 gas stipend)

preconditions: Sender is a smart contract wallet whose receive()/fallback needs >2300 gas or reverts
User attempts self-whitelist by sending ETH to Whitelist

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ETH refund via transfer() rather than call()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: self-whitelist reverts for many contract wallets
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative refund mechanism; no try/catch to still whitelist even if refund fails

analysis_questions: Is self-whitelisting intended to support multisigs/smart accounts?
Could refund failure be safely ignored (keep ETH) or refunded via call with reentrancy-safe pattern?
Is there any path where ETH gets stuck if transfer fails (currently entire receive reverts)?
validation_requirements: Test with a Gnosis Safe / ERC-4337-style smart account sending ETH to Whitelist
Assess whether changing to call introduces reentrancy concerns (state change happens before refund)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not a theft vector; practical DoS: contract wallet users cannot self-whitelist, reducing participation and possibly preventing expected distribution.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect (distribution/participation disruption); could materially affect launch mechanics
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires owner to whitelist manually or redeploy/fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for contract-wallet self-whitelisting

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: repro with a contract that reverts in receive()

threats.title: Multicall.delegatecall to self may allow bypass of intended per-call invariants when combining state transitions atomically
threats.hypothesis_description: Multicall performs delegatecall to address(this) for arbitrary calldata slices. If any state-dependent checks assume 'single function per transaction' (e.g., refundTriggered toggling once per tx, or sequencing assumptions around buy/claimRefund/claimProjectRefund in derived contracts not shown), an attacker can atomically chain calls to reach unexpected intermediate states. While delegatecall preserves msg.sender, it can still create novel state interleavings not covered by individual function reasoning.
threats.investigation_rationale: Delegatecall-based multicall frequently expands the reachable state space. Given ILOPool has lifecycle booleans (_launchSucceeded/_refundTriggered) and refund modifier that mutates state on first entry, atomic call bundling could expose logic gaps (especially around refundable() setting _refundTriggered then executing operations).

proof_of_signal.pattern: for-loop delegatecall to address(this) with user-controlled calldata

preconditions: At least two externally callable functions exist on the same contract that can be combined for unexpected state interleavings
No explicit restriction on multicall usage during sensitive lifecycle phases

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: arbitrary delegatecall batching without whitelist of selectors
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unexpected atomic sequencing across lifecycle transitions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No selector allowlist; no reentrancy/phase gating around multicall

analysis_questions: Can multicall reach refundable() twice in one tx to create inconsistent assumptions (e.g., set _refundTriggered then call launch-related functions via manager callbacks)?
Are there any inherited/external functions not shown (from ERC721) whose sequencing with pool functions can corrupt state?
Is multicall intended to be used by end-users or only integrators?
validation_requirements: Enumerate all external/public functions on deployed ILOPool and attempt adversarial multicall sequences
Property-test lifecycle invariants under arbitrary multicall batching

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker uses multicall to execute a sequence such as (1) trigger refundable() state change via claimRefund/claimProjectRefund prechecks, then (2) attempt additional calls that rely on pre-refund assumptions, potentially causing DoS or state corruption if any checks are order-sensitive.
prerequisites: Satisfy timestamp-based gating for refundable/launch windows

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All pool participants if lifecycle can be corrupted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Unclear; may require admin intervention or redeploy

threats.title: ILOManager._initUniV3PoolIfNecessary may allow attacker-chosen initial pool price if pool exists uninitialized (sqrtPriceX96Existing==0)
threats.hypothesis_description: When a Uniswap V3 pool already exists but slot0.sqrtPriceX96Existing == 0, ILOManager initializes it with the caller-provided sqrtPriceX96. If an attacker can cause a pool to exist in an uninitialized state (e.g., factory created pool but never initialized), the first initProject caller dictates initial price. That can affect downstream assumptions (launch price equality checks and sale economics).
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly allows initializing an existing pool with provided price when uninitialized. While creating such a pool state may be difficult on mainnet (Uniswap V3 pool initialization rules), this is worth validating because the protocol uses the pool address as project ID and uses initial price as a hard equality constraint at launch.
deployment_assumptions: UNIV3_FACTORY is a UniswapV3Factory-compatible contract where pools can exist with slot0.sqrtPriceX96 == 0
An attacker can create the pool before the legitimate project calls initProject, leaving it uninitialized
threats.config_evidence: ILOManager.initialize sets UNIV3_FACTORY from env; no verification it is canonical Uniswap factory or that pool initialization rules prevent uninitialized pools from persisting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if pool exists and sqrtPriceX96Existing==0 then initialize(sqrtPriceX96) with caller-supplied value

preconditions: A pool for (saleToken, raiseToken, fee) exists with sqrtPriceX96Existing == 0
Attacker calls initProject first with chosen initialPoolPriceX96

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: first caller can set initial price in certain factory/pool states
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No restriction on who can initProject; no allowlist of projects

analysis_questions: Under canonical Uniswap V3, can an attacker create a pool and leave it uninitialized such that sqrtPriceX96==0 persists?
If yes, can the attacker set a price that disadvantages later ILO participants or blocks launch?

Simulate pool creation then initProject initialization sequence in fork tests

Launch later fails due to strict equality price checks or causes unfair sale economics.

threats.title: Initializable.whenNotInitialized lacks revert messages and may mask incorrect initialization flows across clones/manager
threats.hypothesis_description: Custom Initializable uses a single boolean _initialized with silent require(!_initialized) and sets it true after function body. This is fragile in multi-contract initialization flows: any accidental call to initialize-like functions can permanently lock future initialization with no clear error. For clones, if any function accidentally uses whenNotInitialized or _disableInitialize is misapplied, the pool/manager can become uninitializable and unusable.
threats.investigation_rationale: Initialization bugs are a common real-world cause of bricked deployments. While not a typical attacker theft vector, a single mis-call (by integrators, factories, or scripts) can permanently DoS core functionality. Given both ILOManager and ILOPool rely on this custom pattern, it warrants deeper validation against deployment scripts and possible cross-calls (e.g., via Multicall).
deployment_assumptions: There exists any callable path that can set _initialized unexpectedly before the intended initialize is executed (e.g., wrong contract address used, accidental call, or multicall usage)
Operational processes might interact with implementation contracts directly instead of clones
threats.config_evidence: Deploy.s.sol deploys ILOPool implementation and calls _disableInitialize() in its constructor, but clone initialization correctness depends on using clone addresses; no guardrails prevent interacting with implementation directly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: single bool guard; require(!_initialized) without message; sets _initialized after executing function body

preconditions: A call sets _initialized=true before intended initialization (e.g., wrong call ordering, wrong address, or unintended function reuse)
No recovery method exists to reset initialization

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: minimal custom initializer guard with global boolean
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bricking risk and opaque failures across deployment/ops
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No versioned initializers; no explicit initializer event; no error messages

analysis_questions: Are there any other functions in codebase using whenNotInitialized besides ILOManager.initialize and ILOPool.initialize?
Do deployment scripts ever call initialize on implementation rather than clones?
Can Multicall be used to call initialize (should revert after initialized) and does that create any side-effects before revert?

Simulate mis-initialization scenarios and ensure no funds can be sent before proper initialization
Verify events/logging to diagnose init state on-chain

prerequisites: Operational/configuration mistake or unexpected call sequence

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol operations can be bricked; funds sent to unusable instances may be stuck
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of affected pool/manager instance

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for initialization misuse scenarios

threats.title: claim() Collects ALL Uniswap Position Fees, Stealing Other Users' Shares and Causing Permanent DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.claim() calls pool.collect(type(uint128).max) which sweeps ALL accumulated fees for the entire shared Uniswap V3 position. User's calculated fee share (amount0/amount1) is sent to the token owner, and the remainder (amountCollected - amount) is sent to FEE_TAKER. Since the remainder includes other users' uncollected fees, those fees are permanently stolen. When subsequent users claim, their calculated fee amounts exceed what collect() returns (because earlier collect swept everything), causing underflow at `amountCollected0-amount0` in Solidity 0.7.6 (no overflow checks), wrapping to ~2^256, and reverting the transfer. All subsequent claims are permanently DoS'd.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting error in how a shared Uniswap V3 position's fees are distributed. Every claim after the first one during a period of fee accumulation will fail, permanently locking user funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.collect(address(this), TICK_LOWER, TICK_UPPER, type(uint128).max, type(uint128).max) collects ALL owed tokens for shared position; then amountCollected0-amount0 sent to feeTaker where amount0 may include fees not backed by collected tokens for later claimers

preconditions: Pool has been successfully launched (_launchSucceeded == true)
Trading has occurred generating fees on the Uniswap V3 position
Multiple users hold NFT positions with vested liquidity to claim

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: collect(type(uint128).max) sweeps entire position's tokensOwed; fee taker transfer: amountCollected0 - amount0 includes other users' fees
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First claimer triggers collection of all accumulated fees; fee taker receives other users' portions; subsequent claims revert due to underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: FEE_TAKER receives 100% of non-claiming users' accumulated trading fees as windfall; all other users permanently lose access to both fees and vested liquidity

economic_questions: What is the total fee accumulation rate for typical ILO pool positions?

threats.title: Oracle TWAP Period Fallback to Near-Spot Price Enables Whitelist Cap Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: UniswapV3Oracle.peek() uses `period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod`. threats.title: ILOManager Constructor Uses tx.origin for Ownership Creating Deployment Security Gap
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager constructor calls `transferOwnership(tx.origin)`. When deployed via a factory, Create2 deployer, multisig, or deployment pipeline, tx.origin is the EOA that initiated the outer transaction, not necessarily the intended owner. Between deployment and initialize() (which transfers ownership to initialOwner), the tx.origin address has full owner privileges and can call setPlatformFee, setPerformanceFee, setFeeTaker, setILOPoolImplementation, etc. The deployment script (Deploy.s.sol) deploys and initializes in the same broadcast, but if initialize() fails or is called separately, the window exists.
threats.investigation_rationale: tx.origin-based ownership is a known anti-pattern. The deployment script partially mitigates by initializing in the same transaction, but separate deployment/initialization (Init.s.sol exists as separate script) creates a real window.

threats.title: Early whitelist phase can be fully DoSed if oracle consult period becomes 0 (no observations), reverting all pool buys

threats.title: No Fee Upper Bound Validation Allows Owner to Set Fees >= BPS Causing Permanent Claim DoS via Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setPlatformFee() and setPerformanceFee() accept any uint16 value without upper bound validation. Fees are captured at project creation in _cachedProject. If fee >= 10000 BPS, the _deductFees function computes amount - mulDiv(amount, fee, BPS) where mulDiv(amount, 10001, 10000) > amount, causing an underflow in Solidity 0.7.6 (no overflow checks). This wraps to a huge value, and subsequent token transfer fails, permanently DoSing all claim() calls for the project.
threats.investigation_rationale: uint16 can hold values up to 65535. setPlatformFee and setPerformanceFee have no require statement bounding the value. _deductFees performs unchecked subtraction in Solidity 0.7.6.

A new project is initialized (capturing the fee at creation time)

threats.title: Vultisig.approveAndCall enables arbitrary external call after granting allowance, allowing immediate token drain within callback
threats.hypothesis_description: approveAndCall() first sets allowance for `spender`, then calls `spender.receiveApproval(...)`. A malicious spender contract can use the freshly granted allowance to call transferFrom and drain the approved amount during the callback, and can chain additional calls (including reentrant patterns into other protocols). While this requires user interaction, it is a practical footgun in production as it creates a 'single-tx approve then pull' primitive with no on-chain limit enforcement beyond the approved amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: approve-and-call patterns are historically high-risk because they combine approval with arbitrary external execution. The code provides no guardrails such as spender allowlisting, EIP-2612-style signature scoping, or reentrancy protection around side effects that a receiver might exploit (e.g., interacting with other contracts in the same tx).

proof_of_signal.pattern: _approve(msg.sender, spender, amount) followed by external call to spender.receiveApproval(...)

threats.title: Owner Can Set Fees Above BPS Causing Underflow DoS on All Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setPlatformFee() and setPerformanceFee() have no upper bound validation. In Solidity 0.7.6, if feeBPS >= 10000 (BPS), _deductFees computes `amount0 - FullMath.mulDiv(amount0, feeBPS, BPS)` which underflows (no overflow checks), producing ~2^256. This massive value causes subsequent token transfers to revert, permanently DoSing all claims for projects initialized with the excessive fee. Fees are snapshot at project creation time.
threats.investigation_rationale: While uint16 caps at 65535, values from 10000-65535 all cause underflow. Any new project initialized after a fee change inherits the broken fee. This is a missing validation that enables permanent fund locking.

threats.title: Missing Fee Upper Bound Validation Allows Owner to DoS All Claims via Underflow in _deductFees
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setPlatformFee and setPerformanceFee accept any uint16 value (up to 65535) with no upper bound check. If PLATFORM_FEE is set >= 10000 BPS (100%), _deductFees computes: amount0Left = amount0 - FullMath.mulDiv(amount0, feeBPS, 10000). When feeBPS >= 10000, the subtracted value >= amount0, causing underflow in Solidity 0.7.6 (no overflow protection). Fees are snapshot at project creation time in _cachedProject, so this affects all pools created after the fee change.
threats.investigation_rationale: uint16 can hold values up to 65535 but BPS should be < 10000. No validation exists, and the fee is applied in unchecked arithmetic.

threats.title: Ownership mis-assignment risk: ILOManager constructor sets owner to tx.origin, enabling unintended privileged actions before initialize()
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager constructor calls transferOwnership(tx.origin). If deployment is performed through a contract, relayer, or multisig that does not have tx.origin equal to the intended owner, ownership may be assigned unexpectedly. Additionally, there is a window before initialize() is called where the tx.origin owner can call onlyOwner functions to set feeTaker/implementation/deadlines maliciously.

It is not an on-chain unprivileged exploit, but it is a plausible operational compromise vector with measurable impact (privileged configuration corruption).

threats.title: ILOPool._unlockedLiquidity() uses unchecked multiplication in Solidity 0.7.6; large totalLiquidity can silently overflow and distort vesting
threats.hypothesis_description: In Solidity 0.7.6 arithmetic does not auto-revert on overflow. In _unlockedLiquidity(), the expression vest.shares * totalLiquidity is evaluated before being passed to FullMath.mulDiv. If the multiplication occurs in a narrower type context or overflows uint256 depending on implicit casting, unlocked liquidity can be incorrect (either lower or higher), potentially allowing over-claim or permanent under-claim for extreme liquidity sizes.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a correctness risk specific to 0.7.6. While extreme values may be required, liquidity values can become large in Uniswap V3, and correctness of vesting math is core to solvency and user trust.
deployment_assumptions: totalLiquidity for some position becomes sufficiently large that vest.shares * totalLiquidity overflows the intermediate arithmetic

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between deployment tx and initialize tx

operational_questions: Are deployment and initialize executed in a single transaction or multiple?
Who controls tx.origin during deployment on the target chain (EOA, multisig module, relayer)?
validation_requirements: Reproduce deployment via the actual pipeline and inspect owner() between deployment and initialization
Attempt onlyOwner calls in between to confirm practical window

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe manager deployed but not yet initialized
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Use temporary ownership (tx.origin) to call onlyOwner setters (e.g., setFeeTaker)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Allow initialization to proceed, masking earlier configuration changes
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires being (or controlling) tx.origin EOA used in deployment

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Medium: temporary owner can alter key parameters affecting future pools
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: misconfiguration may impact fees, refund deadlines, or pool implementations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: can reset parameters if detected; damage may persist for already-created pools
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Global configuration and future pool deployments

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: onlyOwner config changes occurring before initialize() event/logs (if any)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected owner address at deployment time
